text: Lesson 10
AI Literacy for Public Health Staff
textPreview: AI Literacy for Public Health Staff

text: Staff Training Module
AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments
textPreview: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments

alt: AI Playbook for Public Health logo

text:
Welcome participants to the AI Literacy for Public Health Staff lesson.
Explain that this deck is designed for staff training, leadership orientation, governance preparation, or cross-program discussion.
Frame the topic in plain language: AI literacy means staff can recognize AI use, understand major failure modes, protect sensitive data, communicate uncertainty, document use, and know when not to use AI.
Invite participants to think about where this topic already appears in their department's workflows, vendor tools, data systems, communications, or planning conversations.
AI PLAYBOOK AND TOOLKIT
Why this topic matters
AI literacy means staff can recognize AI use, understand major failure modes, protect sensitive data, communicate uncertainty, document use, and know when not to use AI.
textPreview: AI literacy means staff can recognize AI use, understand major failure modes, protect sensitive data, communicate uncertainty, document u...

text: Public health value

AI literacy is necessary for safe use at every level of the department. Staff need role-appropriate knowledge before they are asked to use AI tools, review outputs, approve vendors, participate in governance, or explain AI to the public.
textPreview: AI literacy is necessary for safe use at every level of the department. Staff need role-appropriate knowledge before they are asked to us...

text: Guardrail
Staff should not be asked to use AI tools before they understand privacy, hallucination, bias, documentation, and human review expectations.
textPreview: Staff should not be asked to use AI tools before they understand privacy, hallucination, bias, documentation, and human review expectations.

Ask participants how this topic could affect their own program area, staff roles, community relationships, data, or public communications.

Use this slide in the AI Literacy for Public Health Staff training module.
Walk through the objectives and connect each one to a practical implementation decision.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 5, 6, 10 and Tools 27, 24, 26, 29.
Explain that participants do not need technical expertise to use this material. The goal is enough shared understanding to participate in governance, readiness assessment, use case review, procurement, deployment, or monitoring.
AI PLAYBOOK AND TOOLKIT
Module overview

AI literacy is a workforce capability. Staff should know what AI is, what it is approved for, what data may be used, how outputs can fail, how to document AI use, and when to escalate concerns. Literacy expectations should be role-based because leaders, program staff, analysts, IT, privacy, procurement, communications, and governance members have different responsibilities.
textPreview: AI literacy is a workforce capability. Staff should know what AI is, what it is approved for, what data may be used, how outputs can fail...

text: Key definition

What AI Literacy Means: AI literacy is the ability to recognize AI use, understand what a system can and cannot do, evaluate outputs critically, protect sensitive information, communicate uncertainty, and know when AI should not be used.
textPreview: What AI Literacy Means: AI literacy is the ability to recognize AI use, understand what a system can and cannot do, evaluate outputs crit...

AI PLAYBOOK AND TOOLKIT
What this means in practice
Core Competencies

Recognize approved and unapproved AI use. Protect PHI, confidential, restricted, and sensitive data. Identify hallucination, bias, automation bias, data quality problems, and inappropriate use. Use prompts, source materials, and review steps.
textPreview: Recognize approved and unapproved AI use. Protect PHI, confidential, restricted, and sensitive data. Identify hallucination, bias, automa...

text: Role-Based Training

Executives need governance, risk, resource, and public accountability orientation. Program staff need workflow, privacy, prompt, and review guidance. Analysts need validation, documentation, model monitoring, and data quality practices..
textPreview: Executives need governance, risk, resource, and public accountability orientation. Program staff need workflow, privacy, prompt, and revi...

text: Sustainment
Refresh training as tools, policies, and risks change. Create reporting pathways for staff concerns and near misses. Use AI champions or bridge professionals to support day-to-day adoption.
textPreview: Refresh training as tools, policies, and risks change. Create reporting pathways for staff concerns and near misses. Use AI champions or ...

Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 5, 6, 10 and Tools 27, 24, 26, 29.
For each example, ask: What problem is being solved? What data would be used? Who reviews the AI output? What decision remains human? What could go wrong?
AI PLAYBOOK AND TOOLKIT
Facilitated discussion and exercise
Discussion questions

What does each role need to know to use or oversee AI responsibly? How will the department train new staff and refresh training over time? How will staff report concerns, near misses, or unsafe outputs?
textPreview: What does each role need to know to use or oversee AI responsibly? How will the department train new staff and refresh training over time...

text: Training exercise
Create a role-based training matrix for executives, program staff, analysts, IT, privacy, procurement, communications, and governance members.
